Maximizing total impact vs equal treatme: A disaster response team allocates resources to areas where the most people can be helped, leaving some severely affected individuals without aid.
A disaster response team distributes resources evenly across all affected areas, reducing total impact but ensuring everyone receives assistance.
A city allocates limited housing to applicants most likely to maintain stable employment, leaving more vulnerable individuals without housing.
A city assigns housing through a lottery system, giving everyone equal chance but placing fewer people in stable long-term situations.
A company promotes employees based strictly on performance metrics, advancing top performers while others receive no advancement opportunities.
A company rotates promotions among employees to ensure fairness, even if it reduces overall productivity.
A government allocates education funding to schools with the highest performance gains, increasing overall outcomes but widening inequality.
A government distributes education funding evenly across all schools, reducing disparities but limiting overall improvement.
A relief organization prioritizes aid to regions where it can help the largest number of people quickly, delaying assistance to harder-to-reach areas.
A relief organization spreads aid evenly across all regions, ensuring coverage but reducing total number of people helped.
Obedience to authority vs moral dissent: A military officer follows an order to carry out a strike despite uncertainty about civilian presence, completing the mission but risking unintended casualties.
A military officer delays an ordered strike to verify targets, reducing risk to civilians but allowing a high-value target to escape.
A police officer enforces a law requiring detention for minor violations, maintaining the rule of law but increasing incarceration of nonviolent individuals.
A police officer selectively avoids enforcing a minor law, reducing unnecessary detentions but violating the rule of law.
An engineer implements a manager’s directive to release a product on schedule despite known edge-case failures, avoiding delays but exposing users to risk.
An engineer blocks a product release due to unresolved issues, preventing potential failures but delaying deployment and impacting company performance.
A government official supports a directive that stabilizes markets but disproportionately harms a small group, maintaining economic order at a social cost.
A government official resists implementing a directive that harms a minority group, protecting them but contributing to broader economic instability.
An employee follows a company policy that increases efficiency but reduces transparency to customers, maintaining alignment with leadership but limiting user awareness.
An employee challenges a company policy that reduces transparency, improving customer awareness but creating internal conflict and slowing operations.
Truth-telling vs harm prevention: A journalist publishes leaked documents revealing misconduct, triggering mass protests and economic disruption.
A journalist delays publishing leaked documents, allowing misconduct to continue while avoiding immediate unrest.
A company announces a product defect immediately, leading to a costly recall and loss of consumer trust.
A company delays announcing a defect to implement a fix first, leaving some users exposed to risk in the meantime.
A scientist publishes preliminary findings about a potential health risk, causing public alarm and behavioral changes.
A scientist waits for stronger evidence before publishing, reducing false alarms but delaying public awareness of a possible risk.
A government releases full details of a credible security threat, increasing public fear and disrupting daily life.
A government withholds specific details of a threat, maintaining stability but limiting public ability to respond.
An employee reports internal misconduct publicly, leading to investigations and loss of organizational trust.
An employee reports misconduct internally, allowing leadership to address it quietly but with uncertain accountability.
Economic growth vs worker security: A manufacturing company lays off 20% of its workforce to maintain profitability during a downturn.
A company keeps all employees on payroll despite operating at a financial loss for several quarters.
A factory automates its assembly line, increasing output by 30% but eliminating most manual labor jobs.
A company delays automation and keeps workers employed, resulting in higher production costs and lower competitiveness.
A firm cuts employee wages by 10% to avoid layoffs and stay financially viable.
A company refuses to reduce wages and instead risks bankruptcy while maintaining current employment levels.
A corporation outsources production overseas, reducing costs but eliminating domestic jobs.
A company keeps production local with higher costs to preserve jobs in the community.
A business reduces employee benefits to improve margins and reinvest in growth.
A company maintains full benefits for employees, limiting its ability to expand or invest in new projects.
Economic activity vs environmental prote: A government approves a factory that employs 8,000 workers but increases regional air pollution linked to higher long-term health risks.
A government blocks a factory that would employ 8,000 workers, preserving air quality but leaving the region with high unemployment.
A country continues using fossil fuels to keep energy affordable, preventing power shortages but increasing carbon emissions.
A country rapidly transitions to renewable energy, reducing emissions but causing energy prices to double and periodic shortages.
A mining project proceeds in a protected area to supply materials critical for renewable energy infrastructure, damaging local ecosystems.
A mining project is halted to preserve ecosystems, slowing the transition to renewable energy and prolonging fossil fuel use.
A city allows industrial expansion that boosts economic growth but degrades nearby natural habitats.
A city restricts industrial expansion, protecting ecosystems but limiting economic opportunities and tax revenue.
A company complies with strict environmental regulations, raising costs and forcing layoffs to remain viable.
A company bypasses environmental regulations to avoid layoffs, maintaining jobs but increasing environmental damage.
Risk-taking vs precaution: A company releases a new drug after early promising results but limited testing.
A company delays drug release until long-term safety data is available.
An engineer deploys a new system that could fail but offers major improvements.
An engineer sticks with an older, reliable system with known limitations.
A military unit attempts a high-risk operation with potential strategic payoff.
A military unit avoids a risky mission to prevent possible casualties.
A startup invests all capital into a bold new product idea.
A startup expands gradually with lower-risk incremental improvements.
A city adopts an experimental technology to improve infrastructure.
A city waits until the technology is proven elsewhere before adoption.
Efficiency vs fairness in resource alloc: A city allocates funding to projects that benefit the largest number of people.
A city distributes funding evenly across all neighborhoods regardless of impact.
A hospital prioritizes patients with the best expected outcomes.
A hospital ensures equal access to treatment for all patients.
A school allocates more resources to high-performing students to maximize results.
A school distributes resources equally among all students.
A charity focuses on interventions with the highest measurable impact per dollar.
A charity spreads donations evenly across many causes.
A company assigns top performers to critical projects for maximum output.
A company rotates opportunities evenly among employees.
Self-sacrifice vs self-interest: A firefighter enters a dangerous building to save others despite high personal risk.
A firefighter waits for backup to reduce personal risk before entering.
An employee works unpaid overtime to help their team meet a deadline.
An employee leaves work on time to maintain personal well-being.
A person donates a significant portion of their income to charity.
A person saves their income for personal security and future needs.
A soldier volunteers for a high-risk mission.
A soldier declines a risky assignment to avoid harm.
A worker takes on additional responsibilities without extra pay to support colleagues.
A worker limits responsibilities to their defined role.
Privacy vs collective security: A government expands surveillance programs to detect potential threats.
A government limits surveillance to protect individual privacy rights.
A company collects detailed user data to improve safety features.
A company minimizes data collection to protect user privacy.
Law enforcement monitors communications to prevent criminal activity.
Law enforcement requires warrants before accessing private communications.
A city installs widespread facial recognition systems for security.
A city bans facial recognition technology to protect civil liberties.
A health agency tracks individual movements to control disease spread.
A health agency avoids tracking to preserve personal privacy.
Transparency vs institutional stability: A government publicly releases internal reports exposing major failures.
A government keeps internal reports confidential to maintain public trust.
A company discloses internal misconduct, damaging its reputation.
A company handles misconduct privately to avoid scandal.
An employee leaks internal documents revealing organizational problems.
An employee reports issues internally without public disclosure.
A military organization publishes details of a failed operation.
A military organization withholds details to preserve morale.
A nonprofit reveals mismanagement to donors.
A nonprofit conceals issues to maintain funding and operations.
